cellar.tastingNotes: Rich aromas of wild black cherry, damp forest floor, and delicate rose petal. Beautiful structured tannins and mineral tension typical of Ribbon Ridge sedimentary soils.
cellar.foodPairing: Roasted duck breast, chanterelle mushroom risotto, or herb-crusted pork tenderloin.
cellar.drinkReason: Ribbon Ridge Pinot at 5 years is just entering its window—bright fruit still dominant but secondary complexity emerging, best enjoyed over the next 6 years.
cellar.scoresNote: No scores on record. Flâneur is a small Oregon producer in the Columbia Gorge AVA, but I have no reliable critic score data for this specific wine and vintage.

cellar.tastingNotes: Expressive red raspberry, wild strawberry, and volcanic Jory soil minerality. Fine-grained silky tannins with hints of baking spice and elegant, persistent length. Biodynamically grown.
cellar.foodPairing: Pan-seared salmon, roasted quail, or duck confit.
cellar.drinkReason: This Dundee Hills Pinot Noir needs 1-2 more years to integrate its tannins and allow the terroir-driven fruit to fully express itself.

cellar.wineName: Whistling Ridge Vineyard Blanc de Gris (Brut Nature)
cellar.tastingNotes: Crisp green pear, white orchard blossom, wet stones, and subtle brioche notes from extended lees contact. Extraordinarily dry, bright, saline, and refreshing sparkling wine disgorged in Dec 2024.
cellar.foodPairing: Freshly shucked oysters, Dungeness crab, or tempura spring vegetables.
cellar.drinkReason: This Brut Nature Blanc de Gris is at peak freshness with vibrant acidity and delicate oxidative complexity typical of zero-dosage Oregon sparkling, best enjoyed within the next two years.

cellar.tastingNotes: From dry-farmed vines planted in 1990. Elegant bouquet of red currants, sour cherries, black tea, dried thyme, and iron-like minerality. Classic high-acid structure with superb cellaring potential.
cellar.foodPairing: Roasted lamb chops, wild mushroom ragu, or game hens.
cellar.drinkReason: This Ribbon Ridge Pinot Noir needs 2–3 years to integrate its tannins and allow tertiary complexity to emerge.

cellar.tastingNotes: Exceptional tension and driving energy. Crisp green apple, lemon zest, flinty reduction, and white floral overtones. Cultivated from vines planted in 1993.
cellar.foodPairing: Grilled halibut, roasted chicken with garlic and lemon, or fresh goat cheese.
cellar.drinkReason: This Burgundian-style Willamette Chardonnay needs 1–2 more years to integrate its oak and develop tertiary complexity.

cellar.tastingNotes: Rich biodynamically grown Chardonnay offering creamy lemon curd, white peach, crushed oyster shells, and masterfully integrated subtle French oak spice. Vibrant yet texturally complete.
cellar.foodPairing: Butter-poached lobster, pan-roasted scallops, or creamy chanterelle pasta.
cellar.drinkReason: This Dundee Hills Chardonnay from the cool-climate Willamette Valley needs 1–2 years to integrate its oak and acidity; expect a peak window showcasing bright apple, citrus, and subtle Burgundian minerality. Drink between 2027 and 2033, with the sweet spot around 2028–2031.

cellar.wineName: Maresh Vineyard Pinot Noir Star-of-Bethlehem Flower Block
cellar.tastingNotes: Expect a classically styled Willamette Valley Pinot Noir with bright red fruit — cherry, raspberry, and pomegranate — layered with earthy forest floor, dried herbs, and subtle floral notes. The Star-of-Bethlehem block designation suggests precision and terroir-driven elegance, with silky tannins, vibrant acidity, and a long, mineral-tinged finish.
cellar.foodPairing: Roasted duck breast, salmon with pinot noir reduction, mushroom risotto, charcuterie with aged cheeses, or lamb loin chops with herbs
cellar.scoresNote: No scores on record for this specific single-block bottling. Kelley Fox Wines receives critical attention but I cannot confirm reliable scores for this precise 2019 Maresh Vineyard Star-of-Bethlehem Flower Block bottling without risking fabrication.
cellar.drinkReason: This single-vineyard Dundee Hills Pinot Noir from the volcanic Jory soils of Maresh is still integrating its tannins and primary fruit at 7 years of age; give it another 1–2 years to open fully. At peak it will offer translucent red fruit, forest floor complexity, and the silky texture Kelley Fox is known for, drinking beautifully through the mid-2030s.

cellar.tastingNotes: Expect a textural, layered Pinot Blanc with notes of white peach, pear, Meyer lemon, and subtle floral aromas. Kelley Fox is known for minimal-intervention, terroir-driven wines, so anticipate a vibrant natural acidity, slight savory minerality from the Dundee Hills volcanic Jory soil, and a creamy yet energetic mid-palate.
cellar.foodPairing: Excellent with Pacific Northwest Dungeness crab, pan-seared halibut, roasted chicken with herbs, creamy risotto, aged Gruyère, or mushroom-based dishes.
cellar.drinkReason: Oregon Pinot Blanc is best enjoyed young and fresh, with peak aromatics and acidity over the next 1–3 years.

cellar.tastingNotes: Expect aromas of red cherry, raspberry, dried rose petal, and subtle earthy undertones with hints of forest floor and spice. On the palate, elegant and silky with bright acidity, medium body, fine-grained tannins, and a long, mineral-driven finish.
cellar.foodPairing: Salmon, duck breast, mushroom risotto, roasted chicken, pinot-friendly charcuterie boards, and soft-rind cheeses like Brie or Camembert.
cellar.drinkReason: This Ribbon Ridge Pinot Noir needs 1–2 years to integrate its tannins and allow the bright red fruit and earthy complexity to harmonize. At its peak, expect silky texture with notes of cherry, forest floor, and subtle spice.

cellar.tastingNotes: Ribbon Ridge Pinot Noir from a single vineyard source typically expresses dark cherry, raspberry, earthy forest floor, subtle spice, and silky tannins with bright acidity.
cellar.foodPairing: Salmon, duck breast, mushroom risotto, lamb chops, soft-ripened cheeses
cellar.drinkReason: Ribbon Ridge Pinot Noir from a cool, structured vintage like 2021 typically hits its stride around 4–5 years post-harvest, offering vibrant fruit balanced by developing earthy complexity now through the late decade.
cellar.scoresNote: No scores on record for this specific wine and vintage with sufficient confidence.

cellar.region: Dundee Hills AVA, Willamette Valley, Oregon
cellar.tastingNotes: This single-vineyard Chardonnay from the Dundee Hills AVA, aged 11 months sur lie in barrel, likely exhibits a refined, Burgundian-leaning profile. Expect notes of white peach, green apple, lemon curd, subtle toasted oak, brioche, and a creamy texture from extended lees contact. The cool Willamette Valley climate preserves bright natural acidity, lending excellent tension and minerality to the palate. With only 200 cases produced, this is a highly limited, terroir-driven expression.
cellar.foodPairing: Roasted salmon, halibut in beurre blanc, mushroom risotto, soft-ripened cheeses like Brie or Camembert, roasted chicken with herbs, scallops in cream sauce
cellar.drinkReason: Willamette Valley Chardonnay typically needs 2–3 years to integrate oak and develop complexity before reaching its drinking window.

cellar.tastingNotes: This Willamette Valley Dry Riesling from 2015 likely offers crisp acidity with notes of green apple, lime zest, white peach, and subtle floral aromas. Oregon Rieslings from this region tend to show minerality with a clean, refreshing finish. The dry style keeps residual sugar low, emphasizing vibrant fruit and bright citrus character.
cellar.foodPairing: Excellent with Pacific Northwest seafood such as Dungeness crab, grilled salmon, oysters, sushi, Asian-spiced dishes, goat cheese, roasted chicken, and light vegetable preparations.

cellar.tastingNotes: Expect a cool-climate Pinot Noir with bright red fruit (cherry, raspberry, cranberry), earthy undertones, subtle floral notes, and delicate spice. The Columbia Gorge AVA's volcanic soils and temperature swings lend vibrant acidity and elegant structure with silky tannins.
cellar.foodPairing: Roasted salmon, duck breast, mushroom risotto, charcuterie, pinot-friendly cheeses like brie or gruyère

cellar.tastingNotes: This unfined and unfiltered blend of Dolcetto and Pinot Noir from the Columbia Gorge likely offers bright red and dark cherry fruit, floral violet notes from the Dolcetto, earthy undertones, and silky tannins from the Pinot Noir. Expect refreshing acidity, light-to-medium body, and a rustic, natural character consistent with the minimal-intervention winemaking approach.
cellar.foodPairing: Charcuterie and cured meats, roasted duck, mushroom risotto, grilled salmon, aged sheep's milk cheese, or light pasta dishes with tomato-based sauces.

cellar.tastingNotes: From head-trained vines planted in 1979 at 1050 feet elevation, dry farmed with no tillage, unfined and unfiltered. Expect a wine of great terroir expression with notes of white peach, apricot, petrol, slate minerality, and citrus zest. The old-vine character and natural winemaking approach likely yield a textured, complex dry Riesling with vibrant acidity and impressive depth. Only 24 cases produced, making this an exceptionally rare bottling.
cellar.foodPairing: Roasted pork tenderloin, grilled salmon with herb butter, aged Gruyère, spicy Thai cuisine, roasted root vegetables, or charcuterie boards with aged cheeses.

cellar.tastingNotes: Expect a crisp, light-bodied dry white with notes of green apple, lemon zest, and subtle minerality. The cool Puget Sound climate likely yields high acidity with delicate floral aromatics and a clean, refreshing finish reminiscent of Muscadet.
cellar.foodPairing: Oysters, steamed clams, grilled halibut, fresh goat cheese, light seafood pasta, and Pacific Northwest shellfish

cellar.grapeVariety: Likely Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, Aligoté blend (traditional Crémant de Bourgogne varieties)
cellar.tastingNotes: Expect fresh brioche, green apple, lemon zest, and white flowers with fine persistent bubbles and a clean, crisp finish typical of well-made Crémant de Bourgogne.
cellar.foodPairing: Oysters, goat cheese, charcuterie, smoked salmon, light appetizers, or as an aperitif

cellar.region: Dundee Hills AVA, Willamette Valley, Oregon
cellar.tastingNotes: Single vineyard Burgundian-style Chardonnay fermented and aged sur lie for 11 months in barrel. Expect bright acidity with notes of green apple, lemon curd, white peach, subtle toasted oak, hazelnut, and a creamy, textured mid-palate from extended lees contact. The cool Dundee Hills climate and volcanic Jory soils lend mineral tension and precise focus.
cellar.foodPairing: Dungeness crab, roasted halibut, mushroom risotto, soft-ripened cheeses, roasted chicken with herb butter

cellar.region: Dundee Hills AVA, Willamette Valley, Oregon
cellar.tastingNotes: From a single estate vineyard with 20% whole cluster fermentation and unfined, unfiltered production, expect bright red cherry, wild strawberry, and pomegranate with earthy forest floor, subtle spice, and silky tannins. The whole cluster component adds a savory, stemmy complexity with hints of dried herbs and a long, elegant finish.
cellar.foodPairing: Duck confit, salmon en croûte, mushroom risotto, pinot noir-braised short ribs, aged Burgundy-style cheeses

cellar.tastingNotes: Rich texture and juicy acidity with layers of flavor. Dry-farmed fruit fermented with native yeast, aged in 10% terracotta amphora and 90% neutral oak. Unfined and unfiltered. Sourced from La Belle Promenade Vineyard (Chehalem Mtn. AVA, 100% whole cluster), Jubilee Vineyard (Eola-Amity Hills AVA, 50% whole cluster), and Carson-Phillips (Dundee Hills AVA, 100% destemmed).
cellar.foodPairing: Charcuterie, roasted poultry, mushroom dishes, salmon, light lamb preparations
